data.title: Viral Moments From Artemis II, the Moon Trip for the Digital Age — WSJ
data.description:
By Amira McKee

The Artemis II mission took astronauts farther from the planet than ever before. For the millions following on Earth, the crew has never felt closer.

The record-breaking mission took astronauts around the moon for the first time in over 50 years. This go-round, mission photographs, including those from the far side of the moon, came in near real time. And a livestream gave viewers a glimpse of life in space.

The crew's camaraderie and daily high-jinks took the internet by storm, and turned the four astronauts into international celebrities.

Nutella for mankind

As millions tuned in to watch the Artemis II crew reach their farthest point from Earth, a jar of Nutella drifted into the camera's frame. The floating container of the hazelnut cocoa spread briefly stole attention from the approaching milestone. It became one of the mission's most widely shared images online.

Glam shots

The National Aeronautics and Space Administration approved Apple's iPhone 17 Pro Max for use on the voyage, adding a consumer staple to the Nikon cameras and other devices loaded on the Lockheed Martin-built Orion spacecraft.

While the Artemis II crew has sent a torrent of content back home, the spacecraft's pilot, Victor Glover, said there is more to come.

"There's so much data that you've already seen, but all the good stuff is coming back with us," Glover said during a news conference on April 8. "There's so many more pictures, so many more stories."

Homage to history

Decades after the final Apollo lunar landing in 1972, some rituals haven't changed.

Per NASA tradition, the Artemis II crew didn't take off until the mission's commander, Reid Wiseman, lost a card game. The game: Highest card wins, and the crew played a few rounds until Wiseman was the last one left. The superstition goes that the leader must burn off bad luck, leaving only good fortune for the mission.

As the Orion approached the Moon on April 5, the crew woke up to a message from the Apollo 16 astronaut Charlie Duke. He and John Young landed on the moon in 1972 in a lunar module that was known as Orion.

The next day, the Artemis II crew heard a message from Jim Lovell, the pilot of the first human spaceflight to orbit the moon.

"Welcome to my old neighborhood," Lovell, who died in 2025, said in a recorded audio message. "It's a historic day, and I know how busy you'll be, but don't forget to enjoy the view."

The contemporary Orion carried an American flag originally intended for a canceled Apollo 18 mission.

During the lunar flyby, the crew proposed names for two surface features, continuing a longstanding tradition. In an emotional moment, the crew suggested naming one crater after Wiseman's late wife, Carroll.

Wake-up music

Without day and night in the vacuum of space, morning for Artemis II was marked by a daily wake-up song selected by the crew.

Mission specialist Christina Koch had the first pick and selected "Sleepyhead" by Young & Sick. Also played during the mission were "Under Pressure" by David Bowie and Queen and "Tokyo Drifting" by Denzel Curry and Glass Animals.

When mission control at Johnson Space Center played Chappell Roan's "Pink Pony Club," the song cut just before the titular refrain. After a few seconds of silence, a crew member radioed down, "We were all eagerly awaiting the chorus."

NASA released a playlist of the crew's wake-up tunes on Spotify.

Toilet troubles

The toilet — known as the Universal Waste Management System — caused a few problems during the journey, prompting the crew to use plastic containers to store their liquid waste. At one point, mission controllers were able to work with Koch to fix it.

"I'm proud to call myself the space plumber," Koch said.

Top greetings

During a call with Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney, the astronauts said they shared maple cream cookies while passing the far side of the moon, a nod to astronaut Jeremy Hansen's home country.

President Trump congratulated the crew on breaking the record for deepest human space travel. In a roughly 12-minute call, Trump said the Artemis II mission was laying the groundwork for further expeditions.

"Your mission paves the way for America's return to the lunar surface very soon," Trump said on the April 6 call. "We'll plant our flag once again and this time we won't just leave footprints, we'll establish a permanent presence on the moon and we'll push on to Mars."

Making history

As the Artemis II mission prepared to return to Earth, Koch said there wasn't anything about the voyage she wouldn't miss. The camaraderie among the crew and the teams supporting them on the ground, she said, was a once in a lifetime privilege.

"I will miss being this close with this many people and having a common purpose," Koch said during a news conference on one of the crew's final nights in space. "We are close like brothers and sisters, and that is a privilege we will never have again."

Write to Amira McKee at amira.mckee@wsj.com

data.title: Artemis II Splashes Down Successfully, Spurring the Next Leg of Space Exploration. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

The historic 10-day Artemis II mission to the moon and back is almost finished.

It's been a win for NASA and a win for publicly traded space stocks.

Four astronauts — Americans Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, and Christina Koch, and Canadian Jeremy Hansen — who have traveled farther from Earth than any humans in history, will splash down off the coast of San Diego about 8:07 p.m. Eastern time on Friday, April 10.

They left Earth on April 1 on a mission that energized space enthusiasts. It energized investors too, reminding them that the space economy is real and growing.

Since the launch, through trading on Friday, shares of space technology companies Rocket Lab, Firefly Aerospace, York Space Systems, Redwire, Planet Labs, and AST SpaceMobile were up an average of 23%, about 19 percentage points ahead of the S&P 500.

York dropped 7.5% on Friday, but that left the stock up 50% since the launch.

None of those companies is materially involved in the launch, but that group of six represents the end-to-end space economy, including launch services, parts and satellite supply, and applications including Earth imaging and communications.

As for the mission, the Orion spacecraft carrying the four astronauts will hit the Earth's atmosphere at about 24,000 miles an hour and heat up to roughly 5,000 degrees Fahrenheit. Re-entry is risky, and the ship relies on " Avcoat" tiles to manage all the heat. Avcoat is a mix of silica fibers, phenolic microballoons, and epoxy resin that chars and erodes, removing heat away. A type of Avcoat was used on NASA's Apollo missions.

The re-entry event can be watched live on Barron's above or on YouTube here.

The astronauts' return will bring to a close an epic trip during which American astronauts left low Earth orbit for the first time since the 1970s.

The Artemis group of missions aims to return Americans to the moon and establish a permanent presence there.

Artemis I blasted off in 2022, testing NASA's huge space launch systems, or SLS, and the Orion spacecraft. Artemis II lifted off shortly after 6:35 p.m. Eastern time from the Kennedy Space Center in Cape Canaveral, Fla., on April 1. Artemis III is slated for 2027 and will test Orion and lunar landers built by Elon Musk's SpaceX and Jeff Bezos' Blue Origin. Artemis IV is slated for 2028 and is intended to be the first U.S. moon landing since 1972. Artemis V should go later that year, marking the beginning of moon base construction.

Artemis II was the second flight of the SLS rocket, which has cost tens of billions of dollars to develop. In the time it took to work on SLS, SpaceX essentially perfected its partially reusable Falcon 9 rocket, which has launched roughly 650 times, and dramatically lowered the cost to reach space.

Lower costs have been a boon to the industry. The six commercial space stocks are valued at roughly $100 billion combined. SpaceX, for its part, is valued at about $1.3 trillion and is planning a record-setting IPO for midyear.

While SLS can't compete with SpaceX on costs, the Artemis mission has reminded everyone what is possible when people try to do hard things.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

data.title: Sam Altman's Home Attacked With Molotov Cocktail, Suspect Taken Into Custody — WSJ
data.description:
By Victoria Albert, Meghan Bobrowsky and Zusha Elinson

A person was taken into custody after allegedly throwing a Molotov cocktail at OpenAI Chief Executive Sam Altman's home and making threats at the company's San Francisco headquarters, a spokeswoman for the company said Friday.

The person approached Altman's home in San Francisco's Russian Hill neighborhood around 3:45 a.m. local time and threw an incendiary device toward the property, according to a memo OpenAI sent to employees Friday morning that was viewed by The Wall Street Journal. The device landed nearby and extinguished, and minimal damage was reported, the memo said.

Soon after, someone matching the suspect's description was seen near OpenAI's San Francisco headquarters. That person made threatening statements about the building and was taken into custody by the San Francisco Police Department, the memo said.

The OpenAI spokeswoman said no one was hurt in the incident. In the memo to employees, OpenAI said all of its San Francisco offices remain open Friday and that there is increased police and security presence around the buildings.

News Corp, owner of The Wall Street Journal, has a content-licensing partnership with OpenAI.

The San Francisco Police Department said officers responded to a home at about 4:12 a.m. in connection with a fire investigation, where they learned that a man had thrown an incendiary destructive device, causing a fire at an exterior gate, and fled on foot.

Roughly an hour later, they found the same man outside a business, threatening to burn down a building on the 1400 block of Third Street, and recognized him as the suspect from the earlier incident, the department said. OpenAI's offices are located at 1455 Third St.

A 20-year-old male, who wasn't identified, was arrested, and charges are pending, the department said. It didn't confirm that the home belonged to Altman or that the business targeted was OpenAI.

OpenAI's headquarters on a busy San Francisco thoroughfare has been targeted in the past, according to police reports obtained by the Journal.

Last February, police arrested five demonstrators who had planted themselves in front of the building's entrance and refused to leave. They were part of a larger protest organized by the group Stop AI, which argues that artificial intelligence poses an existential threat to humanity.

A Stop AI spokesperson said the group has no reason to believe the suspect in the Friday incident has ever been associated with it. "Stop AI seeks to protect human life. We do not condone any violence whatsoever," the spokesperson said.

In May, San Francisco police responded to a homeless trespasser who claimed her life was being threatened by "Open AI," according to a police report. When officers arrived, building security officers said they had allowed the woman to sleep on the property, which only became a problem when she went inside the building and refused to leave.

The woman agreed to leave if she could talk with an officer about the company. No arrests were made.

Write to Victoria Albert at victoria.albert@wsj.com, Meghan Bobrowsky at meghan.bobrowsky@wsj.com and Zusha Elinson at zusha.elinson@wsj.com

data.title: CME live cattle set contract highs; hog futures extend slide
data.description:
Live cattle futures on the Chicago Mercantile Exchange set life-of-contract highs on Friday, supported by strong cash markets, tight cattle supplies and continued consumer demand for beef, analysts said.

Most-active CME June live cattle (LCM26) settled up 2 cents at 249.200 cents per pound after posting a contract high at 249.950 cents. The thinly traded April contract (LCJ26) reached 252.250 cents, an all-time high on a continuous chart of the front live cattle contract, before settling at 251.775 cents.

CME May feeder cattle futures (FCK26) rose 1.925 cents to finish at 372.350 cents per pound.

Slaughter-ready cattle traded in Iowa at $250 per hundredweight, traders said on Friday, up about $5 from the previous week's cash price. Meat packers have been paying high prices for cattle due to expectations for a seasonal upswing in beef demand as grilling season approaches, analysts said this week.

Also, packer margins turned positive in late March, stoking demand for cattle, although margins fell back into negative territory this week.

Meanwhile, consumer demand for beef has remained robust, despite elevated prices.

"Even though consumers should be backing away from beef - they should be financially stressed - there is no sign of it yet," said Rich Nelson, chief strategist for Allendale Inc.

U.S. consumer sentiment plunged to a record low in early April and consumers anticipated a surge in inflation in the next 12 months, a monthly survey showed.

Wholesale beef prices ticked lower. The U.S. Department of Agriculture reported choice beef cuts at $380.90 on Friday afternoon, down 19 cents from Thursday.

CME lean hog futures closed lower for a fourth straight session, with the benchmark June contract (LHM26) settling down 0.400 cent at 103.725 cents per pound.

The USDA quoted the wholesale pork carcass cutout at $98.70 per cwt late on Friday, up $1.32 from Thursday.
